sourceTitle: Build a docs-first culture with a beautiful team wiki, powered by a database

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of converting a team's homepage into a wiki?

options.text: It reduces the amount of storage space the team uses.
options.explanation: A wiki does not affect storage; its purpose is to organize information, not to save space.

options.text: It creates a single source of truth for the team’s information.
options.explanation: A wiki centralises knowledge so everyone can find reliable, up‑to‑date information in one place.

options.text: It enables real‑time chat directly on the homepage.
options.explanation: Chat is a separate feature; a wiki focuses on documentation, not messaging.

options.text: It automatically deploys code changes to production.
options.explanation: Wikis are for documentation, not for code deployment or CI/CD processes.

questions.question: Which Notion template can you start with to build a team wiki?

options.text: Product wiki
options.explanation: The Product wiki template is specifically designed for creating a team wiki and can be duplicated and customised.

options.text: Calendar
options.explanation: A calendar template is for scheduling, not for organising documentation as a wiki.

options.text: Meeting notes
options.explanation: Meeting notes capture discussions; they lack the structural properties of a wiki.

options.text: Personal journal
options.explanation: A personal journal is private and not intended for team‑wide knowledge sharing.

questions.question: Which default property is unique to Notion wikis?

options.text: Owner (person property)
options.explanation: Owner is a standard property for many databases, not exclusive to wikis.

options.text: Tags (multi‑select)
options.explanation: Tags are common across databases; they are not wiki‑specific.

options.text: Last edited date
options.explanation: Last edited date appears in regular databases as well.

options.text: Verification
options.explanation: The Verification property is added automatically to wikis and is not present in ordinary databases.

questions.question: How does the verification property help keep documentation trustworthy?

options.text: It locks the page so no edits can ever be made.
options.explanation: Verification does not prevent edits; it only signals that a review has taken place and sets an expiry date.

options.text: It adds a digital signature from Notion itself.
options.explanation: Verification is managed by the page owner, not by an automatic Notion signature.

options.text: It lets the page owner set an expiry date and Notion notifies the team when a review is needed.
options.explanation: The core purpose of verification is to set a date (or infinite) and receive reminders when the document needs to be checked again.

options.text: It automatically publishes the page to the web when verified.
options.explanation: Verification is about internal trust, not about publishing content publicly.

questions.question: If you want a document to never trigger a verification reminder, what should you do?

options.text: Delete the verification property from the wiki.
options.explanation: The verification property is built‑in; removing it isn’t possible and would break other wiki functionality.

options.text: Set the verification date to today’s date.
options.explanation: Setting the date to today would cause an immediate expiry, not prevent reminders.

options.text: Choose the “infinite” verification option.
options.explanation: Marking a page as infinitely verified tells Notion it never expires, so no reminders are sent.

options.text: Assign the page to multiple owners.
options.explanation: Multiple owners affect who can verify, not whether reminders are generated.

questions.question: Which of the following is a recommended way to view only unverified pages in a wiki?

options.text: Sort the database by Owner.
options.explanation: Sorting by Owner does not isolate unverified pages; it merely groups pages by who owns them.

options.text: Create a filter where the verification property is blank or expired.
options.explanation: Filtering on a blank or expired verification property shows exactly the pages that lack a current verification stamp.

options.text: Group the view by Created date.
options.explanation: Grouping by Created date helps with chronological organization but does not target verification status.

options.text: Change the page icon to a warning symbol.
options.explanation: Changing icons is cosmetic and does not affect how pages are filtered or displayed.

questions.question: To prevent accidental edits by most team members, which permission setting should you apply?

options.text: Give full access to everyone on the team.
options.explanation: Full access allows anyone to edit, which does not protect against accidental changes.

options.text: Set most members to “View only” and give full access only to nominated maintainers.
options.explanation: Limiting edit rights to a few trusted maintainers while others have view‑only access reduces the risk of unwanted edits.

options.text: Remove all permissions so nobody can see the wiki.
options.explanation: Removing permissions defeats the purpose of a shared knowledge base.

options.text: Give edit access to everyone except the page owner.
options.explanation: This still allows many people to edit, and the owner losing edit rights is counter‑productive.

questions.question: How can a wiki be transformed into a public‑facing customer help center?

options.text: Duplicate the wiki page and share the link privately with customers.
options.explanation: Sharing a private link does not make the content publicly searchable or accessible without a Notion account.

options.text: Publish the wiki pages to the web and adjust sharing settings to “Anyone with the link”.
options.explanation: Publishing to the web and setting the appropriate sharing permissions turns the internal wiki into an externally accessible help center.

options.text: Export the wiki as a PDF and upload it to an external website.
options.explanation: Exported PDFs are static and lose the interactive filtering, tagging, and updating capabilities of a live wiki.

options.text: Convert the wiki into a Notion calendar and embed it on the company site.
options.explanation: A calendar view does not provide the structured documentation needed for a help center.

questions.question: What is the key distinction between a regular Notion database and a wiki?

options.text: Wikis cannot have multiple views.
options.explanation: Wikis, like databases, support multiple views (table, board, gallery, etc.).

options.text: Wikis automatically include a verification property.
options.explanation: The verification property is added by default to wikis and is not present in standard databases.

options.text: Databases cannot be filtered or sorted.
options.explanation: Filtering and sorting are core features of all Notion databases, including wikis.

options.text: Wikis do not support tags or multi‑select properties.
options.explanation: Wikis inherit all regular database properties, including tags; the unique addition is verification.

questions.question: How can Notion AI assist when building a team wiki?

options.text: It can write, summarize, and outline documentation for you.
options.explanation: Notion AI offers content generation features such as drafting, summarising, and improving writing, which speeds up wiki creation.

options.text: It automatically changes the wiki’s colour scheme to match your brand.
options.explanation: Design customisation is manual; AI does not auto‑apply brand colours.

options.text: It assigns page owners based on each team member’s workload.
options.explanation: Owner assignment remains a manual decision; AI does not evaluate workload for ownership.

options.text: It deletes duplicate pages to keep the wiki tidy.
options.explanation: While AI can suggest edits, automatic deletion of duplicate pages is not a built‑in feature.
